content.text: Archival and Knowledge Transfer on Sunsetting

When a GenAI service is sunsetted, the team that built it disbands, is reassigned, or moves on. The operational knowledge, architectural decisions, failure modes, and hard-won lessons they carry in their heads are not automatically documented anywhere. If the service is ever rebuilt, if a future service makes the same mistakes, or if a post-mortem requires understanding what the service did — all of that knowledge is gone unless it is deliberately captured before the team is dissolved.
Knowledge transfer at sunsetting is not primarily about documentation compliance. It is about preserving institutional memory so that the organization does not pay twice for the same lessons.

attrs.alt: Knowledge transfer artifact map: four quadrants covering operational knowledge, code and model artifacts, performance and safety evidence, and tacit knowledge transfer

content.text: Operational Knowledge Archival

Operational knowledge is the documentation that allows someone unfamiliar with the service to understand how it worked, why it was built the way it was, and what problems were encountered in production.
Architecture Decision Records

Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) capture the reasoning behind major technical decisions. A service that made non-obvious choices — selecting a specific vector database, choosing to serve a quantized model rather than the full model, implementing a custom safety filter — should have ADRs that explain why, what alternatives were considered, and what tradeoffs were accepted.

ADRs are valuable precisely because they explain the  {'type': 'text', 'text': 'why', 'marks': [{'type': 'italic'}]} {'type': 'text', 'text': ", not the  {'type': 'text', 'text': 'what', 'marks': [{'type': 'italic'}]} {'type': 'text', 'text': '. The code documents what was built. The ADR documents why that choice was made over alternatives — information that is irretrievably lost when the team disperses.'}"}

# ADR-0042: Use ONNX Runtime for production inference instead of native PyTorch

**Date**: 2024-08-15
**Status**: Accepted
**Deciders**: Alice Chen (ML Lead), Bob Smith (Platform), Carol Davis (Product)

## Context

Our inference service was initially deployed using native PyTorch for the forward pass.
In load testing, we observed that torch.compile() provided inconsistent latency improvement
across our model architecture (T5-based with custom attention layers), and CPU fallback
on rare CUDA errors caused 30s+ latency spikes.

## Decision

We migrated the inference path to ONNX Runtime (ORT) with the CUDA Execution Provider.

## Alternatives Considered

1. **Keep native PyTorch** — rejected because torch.compile() showed 8% improvement on
   full batches but 40% regression on single-request latency (our primary use case).
2. **TensorRT direct** — rejected because the custom attention layers required significant
   plugin development effort (estimated 6 weeks); ORT's TRT execution provider handled
   them natively.
3. **Triton Inference Server** — evaluated; added deployment complexity we couldn't
   justify for our traffic volume (~500 RPS peak).

## Consequences

Positive: p99 latency reduced 35%; CUDA error fallback path no longer causes spikes.
Negative: ONNX export must be re-run on each model update; adds 40min to model promotion pipeline.
Risk: ONNX opset upgrades occasionally require re-export of existing models.
Post-Mortem Archive

Every significant incident that the service experienced should have a post-mortem document. These documents are some of the most valuable knowledge artifacts because they describe the failure modes that the team encountered — information that a successor system or team would benefit from knowing.
Before the service is decommissioned, the post-mortems should be:
Consolidated into a single accessible location
(not scattered across Jira tickets, email threads, and Slack channels)
Tagged with the failure mode category
so they can be searched by future teams working on similar systems (e.g., 'GPU OOM', 'model output drift', 'rate limiter bug', 'prompt injection')
Summarized into a 'recurring failure modes' document
that distills the pattern from individual incidents
Runbook Archival

Runbooks for non-obvious operational procedures should be preserved even after the service is shut down. A runbook that describes how to safely restart the inference cluster without causing model state corruption, or how to perform an emergency model rollback, may be directly applicable to successor services.
Code and Infrastructure Archival
Repository Archival
The source code repository should not be deleted when the service is decommissioned. Instead:
Tag the final release with a decommission tag (e.g., decommissioned-20260301) so the state of the code at sunset is permanently recorded
Archive the repository in the version control system (GitHub's archive feature marks it read-only but preserves all history)
Add a prominent README notice indicating the service was decommissioned, when, and what replaced it

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/archive_repository.sh
# Tags the repository at the decommission state and updates the README

SERVICE="genai-inference-service"
SUNSET_DATE=$(date +%Y%m%d)
REPLACEMENT="genai-inference-service-v2"
REPLACEMENT_REPO="https://github.com/company/${REPLACEMENT}"

# Create a decommission tag
git tag -a "decommissioned-${SUNSET_DATE}" \
  -m "Service decommissioned on ${SUNSET_DATE}. Replaced by ${REPLACEMENT_REPO}"
git push origin "decommissioned-${SUNSET_DATE}"

# Update README with decommission notice (prepend to existing README)
NOTICE="## DECOMMISSIONED — ${SUNSET_DATE}

This service was decommissioned on ${SUNSET_DATE}.
Replacement: [${REPLACEMENT}](${REPLACEMENT_REPO})
For historical reference only. This repository is archived and read-only.
Contact platform-team@company.com for questions about the successor service.

---
"
echo "${NOTICE}" | cat - README.md > /tmp/README_new.md
mv /tmp/README_new.md README.md
git add README.md
git commit -m "docs: mark repository as decommissioned (${SUNSET_DATE})"
git push origin main

echo "Repository tagged and updated. Archive in GitHub UI: Settings → Archive repository"
Prompt Template and System Prompt Archival

For GenAI services, the prompt engineering artifacts — system prompts, few-shot examples, chain-of-thought templates, safety instruction blocks — are a category of IP that is easy to overlook. These templates often represent significant iteration and testing effort and are directly reusable in successor systems.
Prompt templates should be archived with version history intact. The final production versions should be explicitly tagged and documented with:
The model they were designed for
(a system prompt tuned for Claude 3.5 Sonnet behaves differently on Gemini 1.5)
The safety considerations they encode
(e.g., 'this template includes an injection-resistant boundary because the service accepts untrusted user content')
The evaluation results
associated with the prompt version

# prompt-archive/system_prompt_v3.yaml
# Final production system prompt archived at decommission
# Model: claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022 (primary) / gpt-4o-2024-11-20 (secondary)
# Active from: 2025-04-01 to 2026-03-01 (decommission date)
# Evaluation: safety_eval_v12.json (PASS), accuracy_eval_v8.json (94.2%)

metadata:
  version: "3.0"
  effective_date: "2025-04-01"
  decommission_date: "2026-03-01"
  authored_by: "ml-safety-team"
  model_family: ["claude-3-5", "gpt-4o"]

safety_notes: >
  Version 3 added the injection-resistant boundary (line 42-47) after red-team
  exercise RE-2025-003 found that v2 was susceptible to role-playing attacks
  in the educational context. The boundary was tested against 2000 adversarial
  prompts with 0 successful bypasses.

evaluation_artifacts:
  safety_eval: "s3://eval-archive/safety_eval_v12.json"
  accuracy_eval: "s3://eval-archive/accuracy_eval_v8.json"
  red_team_report: "s3://eval-archive/red-team-RE-2025-003.pdf"

content: |
  You are a helpful assistant for [service context].
  [... full system prompt ...]
Safety and Performance Evidence Archival

For GenAI services, the safety evaluations conducted during the service's lifetime are regulatory artifacts in some jurisdictions and important institutional knowledge in all cases. A safety regression that was discovered, fixed, and documented is knowledge that prevents the same regression from appearing in a successor system.
Model Cards

Every fine-tuned model that was deployed to production should have a model card archived alongside the model weights. The model card documents the model's intended use cases, known limitations, evaluation results, and safety characteristics.

# Model Card: genai-inference-service/assistant-v2.1

## Model Details

- **Base model**: Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct
- **Fine-tuning method**: LoRA (rank=16, alpha=32)
- **Training data**: 45,000 customer service interactions (2024-Q1 to 2024-Q3)
  - Data was anonymized and de-identified before use
  - Customer consent obtained per privacy policy v3
- **Training infrastructure**: 4x A100 80GB SXM, CUDA 12.3
- **Training date**: 2024-10-15 to 2024-10-22

## Intended Use

Intended for customer support chat for [service category]. Designed to answer
product questions, troubleshoot common issues, and escalate complex issues to
human agents.

**Not intended for**: medical advice, legal advice, financial recommendations,
autonomous decision-making without human review.

## Evaluation Results

| Metric | Score | Baseline (base model) |
|--------|-------|-----------------------|
| Task accuracy (held-out set) | 91.3% | 74.2% |
| Safety eval (harm rate) | 0.2% | 2.1% |
| Refusal precision | 96.8% | 88.4% |
| Bias (gender parity on occupations) | 0.97 | 0.91 |

## Known Limitations

1. Occasionally hallucinates product pricing when the product was launched
   after training data cutoff (Oct 2024). Mitigation: RAG with live catalog.
2. Code generation quality is low — the training data contained few technical
   queries. Do not use for code generation use cases.

## Archive Location

- Weights: s3://model-registry/archive/genai-inference-service/assistant-v2.1/
- Decommission date: 2026-03-01
- Contact: ml-team@company.com
Safety Evaluation Archival
Red team reports, bias assessments, and safety evaluation datasets should be archived in the same S3 location as audit logs. They serve two purposes after decommission:
Regulatory documentation
— the EU AI Act and emerging AI regulations in other jurisdictions may require organizations to retain safety documentation for deployed AI systems
Institutional knowledge
— the failure modes discovered in red team exercises are directly applicable to successor systems and should be fed into the organization's shared safety testing library
Tacit Knowledge Capture

Tacit knowledge is what the team knows that is not written down anywhere. It includes intuitions about when the model behaves unexpectedly, undocumented workarounds for infrastructure quirks, vendor relationship context, and the accumulated judgment about what matters and what does not. This is the hardest category to transfer and the most likely to be lost.
Structured Knowledge Elicitation
A structured retrospective session with the outgoing team is the most effective mechanism for capturing tacit knowledge. The session should be recorded and should cover:
Question Category
Example Questions
Things that surprised us
What behavior of the model/infrastructure was counterintuitive? What did we expect to be easy that was hard?
What we'd do differently
If we rebuilt this tomorrow, what would we change? What decisions do we regret?
Non-obvious failure modes
What failure modes are not documented in post-mortems? What could go wrong that we never wrote down?
Undocumented dependencies
What does this service depend on that is not obvious from the architecture diagram?
Vendor relationships
What do we know about our model provider that isn't in the contract? What SLAs were informally agreed?
Team recommendations
Who should the successor team talk to? What external experts helped us?
Lessons Learned Feed-Back Loop

Knowledge captured during a decommission retrospective has value beyond the immediate context if it is fed back into the organization's platform engineering standards. A lesson learned from decommissioning a GenAI service — for example, 'fine-tuned model weights must be archived with training data provenance documentation from day one, not retroactively during decommission' — can change the standard for future services so the lesson does not need to be re-learned.

# Lessons Learned Feed-Back: genai-inference-service decommission

**Date**: 2026-03-01
**Team**: GenAI inference team (Alice Chen, Bob Smith, Carol Davis)
**Platform Engineering Contact**: Dave Wilson

## Lessons to incorporate into platform standards

### 1. Prompt template versioning from day one
We had 12 prompt template versions in production over the service's 18-month
life, and we had to reconstruct the version history from Git blame and Slack
messages. Every service should use the prompt versioning schema from service
launch, not as a retroactive cleanup task.

**Recommended platform change**: Add prompt versioning to the GenAI service
starter template and make it part of the launch checklist.

### 2. Model card authoring at training time, not at decommission
We spent 3 days reconstructing model cards from training logs and Slack messages.
Model cards should be required before a fine-tuned model is deployed to staging.

**Recommended platform change**: Block promotion from evaluation to staging in
the model promotion pipeline unless a model card is present.

### 3. Vector store content classification
Our RAG vector store contained embeddings of customer documents that we classified
as "derived data" for retention purposes. Legal review during decommission
determined they should be treated as personal data. We had to delete them under
GDPR's 30-day timeline, which required emergency infrastructure work.

**Recommended platform change**: Update the data classification guide to explicitly
categorize vector store embeddings based on source content, not the derived format.
Decommission Archive Structure

All archival artifacts should be organized into a consistent archive structure that makes them discoverable years after the decommission. A flat structure in S3 or a single Confluence space organized by artifact type works better than service-specific deep folder hierarchies.

s3://decommission-archive/
  genai-inference-service/
    manifest.json                     # Index of all artifacts and their locations
    DECOMMISSION-SUMMARY.md           # One-page summary: what it was, why sunsetted, what replaced it
    operational/
      adrs/                           # Architecture Decision Records (all versions)
      runbooks/                       # Final production runbooks
      post-mortems/                   # All incident post-mortems
      failure-modes-summary.md        # Distilled failure mode knowledge
    code/
      source-tag-ref.txt              # Git tag and repo URL for final code state
      infrastructure/                 # IaC snapshots (Terraform state, Helm values)
      prompt-templates/               # Final production prompt templates with metadata
    models/
      model-registry-refs.json        # Pointers to model weights in model registry
      model-cards/                    # Model cards for all deployed models
    safety/
      red-team-reports/               # All red team exercise reports
      bias-assessments/               # Fairness evaluation results
      safety-eval-datasets/           # Test datasets used for safety evaluation
    performance/
      benchmark-history.json          # Performance baselines over service lifetime
      capacity-planning-notes.md      # Scaling characteristics and infrastructure sizing
    knowledge-transfer/
      retrospective-recording.mp4     # Recorded knowledge transfer session
      lessons-learned.md              # Lessons fed back to platform standards
      team-contacts.md                # Who to contact for questions (with current roles)
    compliance/
      deletion-certificate.json       # Data deletion/erasure certificate
      audit-log-archive-ref.txt       # Pointer to audit log archive
      data-export-records/            # Customer data export completion records
Summary

Archival and knowledge transfer at service sunsetting is the final obligation to the organization and to future teams. A service that is shut down without capturing its operational knowledge leaves a knowledge debt — not immediately visible, but paid later when a successor service rediscovers the same failure modes, makes the same architectural mistakes, or cannot comply with a regulatory audit because safety documentation was lost.

The four categories of artifacts — operational knowledge (ADRs, post-mortems, runbooks), code and model artifacts (versioned source, fine-tuned weights, prompt templates), safety and performance evidence (red team reports, model cards, benchmarks), and tacit knowledge (retrospective sessions, lessons learned) — cover both the explicit and implicit knowledge the service embodies.

For GenAI services specifically, the prompt templates, model cards, and safety evaluation records are artifacts that are often not archived because they do not fit neatly into existing software archival practices. Making them first-class archival artifacts — with an explicit location in the archive structure, a required format, and a named owner — prevents the loss that would otherwise occur when the team disperses and the institutional memory goes with them.
